event_name: UFC Fight Night: Fiziev vs. Torres

fights.fighter_a_bio: Fiziev leans on speed and volume striking to rack up points, but his gas tank and chin aren't his strongest assets in longer fights. He'll need to land cleaner shots and control the pace early—otherwise he gets picked apart.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Torres is a massive favorite here (95% by our model) because he consistently pressures opponents, lands heavy, and doesn't fade late. He's proven more durable and effective than Fiziev across the board.
fights.watch_for: Whether Fiziev can survive the first two rounds without absorbing too much damage; if Torres gets rolling with sustained pressure and lands his power shots clean, the fight is effectively over.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Magomedov is a heavy-handed striker who uses footwork and distance control to land clean shots while staying out of range. Our model makes him a strong 83% favorite—he's proven (1557.6 Elo) at picking his spots and finishing strikers who trade with him.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Pereira mixes kicks, hand speed, and occasional wrestling entries to create chaos, but his offense often lacks precision and he abandons game plans mid-fight. He's facing a striker who punishes wild combinations, which is exactly where his unpredictability (0.558 chaos index) becomes a liability rather than an asset.
fights.watch_for: Does Pereira rush in throwing volume early, or does he stay methodical? Magomedov picks apart sloppy approach patterns—if Pereira commits to combinations without setup, he eats clean counters.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Sadykhov throws heavy hands and mixes in takedowns to keep opponents honest—he's not locked into one mode. His Elo (1546) and our model's 69% edge suggest he's proven at executing this balance under pressure.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Camilo hunts submissions and top control; he's most dangerous when he gets you down. At Elo 1483, he's capable but less tested than Sadykhov, and our model gives him only a 31% path to victory.
fights.watch_for: Can Camilo secure a takedown early and threaten a submission before Sadykhov's striking volume forces him to engage on the feet?

fights.fighter_a_bio: Almabayev is a mat technician who hunts takedowns early and controls opponents on the ground with heavy top pressure. His weakness is getting there—if you stuff his shot and keep him standing, his limited striking makes him a sitting duck.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Johnson mixes strikes and grappling to keep opponents guessing, but our model gives him a slight edge (52.5%) because he's proven more effective at adapting mid-fight. He's the one with more ways to finish if Almabayev's gameplan falls apart.
fights.watch_for: Can Almabayev land his first takedown in round 1? If he does, he grinds out control points. If Johnson sprawls and keeps it standing past round 2, Almabayev gasses and loses the striking exchanges.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Aliskerov lands heavy hands from the clinch and uses wrestling to grind opponents against the cage—he's a big favorite here at 79% win probability because he controls pace and limits space. His chain wrestling and upper-body strength make him a nightmare in tight quarters.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Ferreira mixes submissions with striking but hasn't faced pressure like this before—he'll need to stay loose and create angles to avoid getting pinned. His Elo rating suggests he's fought top names, but the model sees Aliskerov's wrestling as a hard counter.
fights.watch_for: Whether Ferreira can stay off the cage in the first round. If Aliskerov gets him clinched early, he'll dictate the fight; if Ferreira circles and makes space, his submission threat becomes real.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Magomedov is a heavy-pressure wrestler who drags opponents into the clinch and grinds them against the cage—he controls pace entirely through top position and suffocating grappling. The model heavily favors him (92.7%) because his wrestling is extremely difficult to escape once locked in.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Oleksiejczuk mixes striking with submission attempts off his back, but he'll be facing a relentless takedown threat that neutralizes his ability to land strikes cleanly. At 7.3% odds, he'd need to stuff takedowns early and land something clean—a tough ask against Magomedov's relentless forward pressure.
fights.watch_for: Whether Oleksiejczuk can defend the initial clinch entries in rounds 1–2; if Magomedov gets him to the mat and holds position, the fight is essentially over.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Farman Hasanov comes in as the model's slight pick (52.3%), though neither fighter has much proven track record at this level. His Elo sits just above Nolan's, suggesting this is a toss-up between two unknowns.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Eric Nolan's 47.7% win probability reflects a marginal gap—he's operating at nearly the same proven level as Hasanov. Expect a competitive fight with real uncertainty baked in.
fights.watch_for: Whoever controls the clinch and dictates positioning early will likely steal rounds; both fighters are unpredictable enough (0.5 chaos index) that a single mistake or submission opportunity could flip the result.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Yakhyaev is a relative unknown stepping into unfamiliar territory—his fight style isn't established, which makes predicting his approach difficult. At an even Elo rating with Walker, he'll need to impose whatever his strongest asset is early before the fight gets away from him.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Walker sits virtually tied with Yakhyaev on paper, but our model gives him a slight 50.2% edge, suggesting something in his fight profile matches up favorably here. Without knowing his specific weapons, he's the marginal pick to execute whatever his gameplan is more effectively.
fights.watch_for: Who controls the pace in rounds 2–3: if Yakhyaev can dictate tempo and force Walker into his world, he wins; if Walker absorbs pressure and imposes his rhythm, the model's pick likely closes out the decision.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Naurdiev pressures with heavy hands and rides a wrestling base to control the center—he's not flashy but accumulates damage through relentless forward movement. Our model heavily favors him (82%) because he's proven at this level and dictates pace rather than chasing
fights.fighter_b_bio: Vettori's a volume striker who banks on cardio and ring IQ to outwork opponents over 15 minutes, but he struggles against pressure fighters who don't respect his output. His 1530 Elo (slightly higher than Naurdiev's) masks the stylistic mismatch here
fights.watch_for: Whether Vettori can circle away and make space for his combinations, or if Naurdiev's pressure forces him to the fence where wrestling becomes an option—if Naurdiev gets his hands on him early, it's a long night for Vettori

fights.fighter_a_bio: Ruziboev is a heavy pressure fighter who stalks opponents into the cage and lands volume strikes from distance before closing. His Elo rating (1547) signals proven success against better competition, and our model gives him a decisive 79.5% edge.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Pulyaev mixes striking and grappling but lacks the output or finishing urgency to match Ruziboev's relentless pace. He'll need to land a clean counter or drag this to the mat to steal rounds.
fights.watch_for: Whether Pulyaev can reverse Ruziboev's forward momentum with takedowns or get comfortable enough to land his own combos—if Ruziboev dictates the entire fight at range, it's a blowout.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Donchenko is a heavy hitter who relies on volume and aggression to overwhelm opponents—he throws a lot of strikes early and forces the pace. He's got limited track record at this level, making him a relative unknown with unpredictable tendencies.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Gustafsson is slightly more proven on paper with a marginally higher Elo rating, suggesting steadier performances over time. Our model gives him a razor-thin 50.6% edge, meaning this is essentially a coin flip.
fights.watch_for: Whether Donchenko's early aggression and striking volume can catch Gustafsson before Gustafsson finds his distance and settles into a more measured, technical approach.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Almakhan is a big favorite here (84% win probability by the model) because he pressures relentlessly—closing distance, firing leg kicks, and grinding opponents down with clinch work and top control. He's proven at this level (1503.6 Elo) and doesn't leave much to chance.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Matsumoto has a higher Elo rating (1534.1) suggesting past success, but the model gives him only 16% to win this one. He'll need to stay at range and use speed; if Almakhan gets inside, it's over.
fights.watch_for: Whether Matsumoto can maintain distance and land clean shots before Almakhan closes the gap and locks him down—if Almakhan gets the clinch or top position, Matsumoto's chances collapse.
